title: C. Stepan and Permutation
problemStatement: আমার x,y দেওয়া থাকবে। লাইনে আমি x,y ঘর সামনে পিছনে গিয়ে arry sort করতে পারবো কিনা
conceptsLearned: x,y এর GCD নিয়ে v[i]%gcd == i%gcd হলে যাবে

title: Doremy's IQ Test
reason: Wanted a harder Div 1 problem to practice binary-searching on the answer instead of the array — this one forced me to find the monotone condition myself.
problemStatement: Given a sequence of queries over a growing prefix, determine for each query whether removing exactly one element still lets the remaining values satisfy a fixed property, where queries are guaranteed to be monotone in a hidden way.
intuition: My first instinct was to look for a closed-form formula relating index parity to feasibility — the small sample cases looked suspiciously periodic.
whyWrong: That fell apart with duplicate values in the array. The periodic pattern was a coincidence of the sample input, not a real property — the actual feasibility depends on cumulative Fenwick-tree state as the search window advances, which a formula can't capture.
conceptsLearned: Binary searching over an index rather than a value, proving monotonicity before trusting a binary search, and maintaining a Fenwick tree incrementally inside a search loop instead of rebuilding it.

title: AND Sorting
reason: Bitmask constructive problems always trip me up — picked this to build intuition for when AND-swaps form equivalence classes.
problemStatement: We may swap two positions i, j only when a_i AND a_j equals some fixed mask k. Find the largest k that still allows fully sorting the array.
intuition: I assumed every out-of-order pair needed to be directly swappable, so I started computing the AND of adjacent inversions only.
whyWrong: That undercounted valid values of k. Swaps chain transitively through intermediate elements, so restricting to adjacent pairs missed cases where a longer chain of swaps still sorted the array correctly.
conceptsLearned: Transitivity in swap-graphs, ANDing a bitmask condition across an entire permutation, and recognizing connected components implied by an operation rather than stated explicitly.
approach:
## Insight

Any two positions that are *out of order* relative to the sorted array must be swappable, directly or transitively. So `k` must be a submask of `a_i AND a_j` for every misplaced pair `(i, j)`. Taking the AND of all such pairs gives the tightest possible `k`.

## Solution

```python
k = (1 << 30) - 1
for i in range(n):
    if a[i] != sorted_a[i]:
        k &= a[i] & sorted_a[i]
print(k)
```

## Why this works

Swapping is transitive when the AND condition holds for a chain of intermediate elements, so we don't need every pair to be directly swappable, just connected through the allowed-swap graph.

title: Watermelon
problemStatement: Given a weight w, decide whether the watermelon can be split into two positive even halves.
intuition: Recognized immediately as a parity check with one edge case to watch for.
conceptsLearned: Careful handling of the single edge case (w = 2) in an otherwise trivial parity check.
approach:
## Approach

An even number `w` can be split into two positive even parts unless `w == 2`. Just check `w % 2 == 0 and w > 2`.

```python
w = int(input())
print("YES" if w % 2 == 0 and w > 2 else "NO")
```

A good warm-up problem, nothing more to it than the parity check.
